metadata.notes: Acceptable-answer sets reference doctors by full name. The evaluation script resolves names to doctors.ID at grading time via the live database, IDs are never hardcoded here.

input.presenting_issue: I went through something really frightening about two months ago and I still think about it a lot, but I don't have nightmares or flashbacks and I'm not avoiding anything because of it. I just want to talk it through with someone and understand it better.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Presenting issue describes trauma-related distress without any of the four PTSD symptom clusters (intrusion, avoidance, negative mood/cognition, hyperarousal) and explicitly denies two of them. Falls under the 'Trauma' label, not 'Trauma and PTSD', per the boundary rule. Trauma is a singleton specialisation, only one doctor treats it.
test_cases.reasoning: Symptom picture matches subclinical trauma per the reference table, not full PTSD. Trauma (as distinct from Trauma and PTSD) is only covered by Nakia Parker. Her approach list (Psychodynamic, Trauma Focused, Relational, Narrative) also fits 'wants to talk it through and understand it', meaning-making language points toward Narrative/Relational work specifically, reinforcing the fit beyond just the specialisation label.

input.presenting_issue: I have not been able to sleep for weeks, I have tried everything but I find myself staying up into the dead of night, my mind just remaining awake making me tired and unable to function during the day, not sure what to do, I could use some help.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: All four elements of the clinical picture for Sleep and Insomnia are present (difficulty sleeping, weeks-long duration, daytime impairment, failed self-help), with no anxiety-cluster language (no worry, no fear-of-not-sleeping) to pull it toward Anxiety or Mood Disorders instead. Sleep and Insomnia is a singleton specialisation, only one doctor treats it.
test_cases.reasoning: Presenting issue reflects sleep-onset insomnia with daytime impairment, matching the reference table's Sleep and Insomnia picture directly, not the sleep-anxiety variant (no fear-of-sleeplessness language), so CBT-I fit over Exposure Therapy specifically. Checked against the full roster: Mood Disorders and Anxiety doctors (Snow, TChalla, Mokoena, Maryland, Obiagu) don't apply since nothing in the presenting issue reflects those symptom clusters. Peter Mercer is the only doctor carrying Sleep and Insomnia at all.

input.presenting_issue: I am always stressed about money, I have deep fears that I will never have enough money and it causes me to work myself overtime, I think it could maybe been related to financial issues in my childhood. Maybe seeing a therapist would be good for me.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Stress and fear are monothematic, confined entirely to money, not generalised, ruling out Anxiety. Overworking is a downstream coping behaviour driven by financial fear, not a work-originated complaint, and none of burnout's defining markers (emotional exhaustion, cynicism toward work, reduced sense of accomplishment) are present, ruling out Workplace Stress and Burnout despite the surface wording overlap. Financial Psychology is a singleton specialisation, only one doctor treats it.
test_cases.reasoning: Financial anxiety with a childhood-scarcity origin and a concrete behavioural consequence (compulsive overwork) matches Financial Psychology's core presentation from the reference table precisely. Checked against Anxiety (Mokoena, Mercer, Maryland, Obiagu) and Workplace Stress and Burnout (Dlamini, Maryland, Stark) doctors specifically given the surface wording risk, neither applies once the causal direction and monothematic focus are accounted for. Dlamini speaks Zulu and offers in-person sessions, so the hard filters don't accidentally exclude her either. Natasha Dlamini is the only doctor carrying Financial Psychology.

input.presenting_issue: We are newlyweds who got married a year ago and we have been fighting non-stop, it feels like our marriage is imploding before it even started.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Type B ambiguity: single specialisation (Relationship Issues) with multiple candidate doctors, and no approach-specific language in the presenting issue favours any one doctor's flavour of treatment. Wells is eliminated by the Couples participant-type hard filter (she only sees Individuals and Family), narrowing the ambiguity from 3 doctors to 2 rather than eliminating it entirely, both remaining doctors (Parker, Khang) stay genuinely tied since neither insight-oriented (Parker) nor family-systems (Khang) language appears in the presenting issue.
test_cases.reasoning: Relational distress and conflict match Relationship Issues directly, with deliberately approach-neutral wording, no insight-oriented, tools-based, or systemic-flavoured language to discriminate between doctors. Checked all three Relationship Issues doctors against the Couples participant-type filter: Wells only sees Individuals and Family, eliminating her before approach reasoning applies. Parker and Khang both see Couples and both remain equally unfavoured by the wording, so both stay in the acceptable-answer set. Confirmed 'No preference' is a valid session-type value on the real intake form before using it here.

input.presenting_issue: I have been feeling so down lately, feels like my chest is on fire because of how much I cry. I just lost my grandfather who I loved dearly and it takes a while for me to get out of bed. It feels like a dark cloud hanging over my head. I don’t know how to handle this pain.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Type A ambiguity: presenting issue genuinely supports both Depression (severity/impairment language, psychomotor slowing, persistent low mood) and Grief (specific, recent, emotionally-anchored loss with pain directly attributed to it) simultaneously, recreating the real clinical distinction between severe/complicated grief and a bereavement-triggered depressive episode. No hard filter narrows the 4-doctor candidate pool since session type and gender preference are both 'No preference'.
test_cases.reasoning: Depression symptoms (down, heavy crying, psychomotor slowing, persistent low mood) and Grief symptoms (specific named loss, strong attachment, pain attributed to the loss) are both fully present, neither diluted to make room for the other. Checked all doctors carrying either specialisation: Wells (both), Maryland (Depression only), Parker and Livingston (Grief only). All four pass Individuals/Adult/English. Session type and gender preference are both 'No preference', so neither narrows the set, all four doctors remain genuinely tied.

input.presenting_issue: I experienced such a scare. I was almost kidnapped at gunpoint, I escaped luckily and I am safe, but I feel so traumatized. I find myself shaking, I dread leaving my house, I can’t cope with this. I need help.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Trauma vs Trauma and PTSD boundary case: two of four DSM-5 symptom clusters present (avoidance: dread leaving the house; hyperarousal: shaking), but no intrusion or negative mood/cognition symptoms, and critically, no duration marker at all. Shaking and avoidance immediately after a severe, armed traumatic event are normal, expected acute stress reactions, only becoming PTSD-consistent if sustained past a month with real impairment. Since duration is unstated, both 'Trauma' and 'Trauma and PTSD' readings are equally defensible. Gender preference (Female, hard filter) eliminates TChalla from the underlying 3-doctor trauma-boundary pool (Parker, TChalla, Govender), narrowing the acceptable-answer set to 2.
test_cases.reasoning: Symptom severity of the precipitating event (armed near-kidnapping) does not by itself indicate PTSD, most people exposed to even severe trauma don't develop the full disorder, so severity and diagnostic threshold were deliberately kept as separate considerations. Checked all three trauma-boundary doctors: Parker (Trauma), TChalla and Govender (Trauma and PTSD). Gender preference is Female and is now a hard filter, eliminating TChalla (Male) before the trauma-boundary reasoning applies. Parker and Govender both pass Individuals/Adult/English/Online.

input.presenting_issue: I got pushed into a pool at a party and nearly drowned, I can't swim. Everyone thought it was funny except one friend who jumped in. I'm still shaken, dread going to school, and haven't told my parents.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Trauma vs Trauma and PTSD boundary case: avoidance (school avoidance, though ambiguously motivated by either trauma-reminder avoidance or social shame from being mocked) and hyperarousal ('still so shaken') are softly present, intrusion and negative mood/cognition are absent, and duration is unstated. Same borderline logic as TC006, sustained vs acute reaction is genuinely undecidable from the text. Unlike TC006, no hard filter narrows the 3-doctor trauma-boundary pool, all of Parker, TChalla, and Govender cover Child/Teen, English, and In-person, and gender preference is 'No preference'.
test_cases.reasoning: Near-drowning with a social betrayal element (peers laughing rather than helping) and secrecy from parents gives real clinical texture, but the described reaction stays soft on cluster language and includes no duration marker, keeping this genuinely borderline rather than clear-cut. Checked all three trauma-boundary doctors against Individuals/Child-Teen/English/In-person, all three pass. No preference stated for gender, so unlike TC006, the full 3-doctor ambiguous pool remains intact, demonstrating that hard filters don't narrow every ambiguous case, only when the stated preferences happen to intersect with a candidate's attributes.

input.presenting_issue: I've felt so low for weeks, nothing brings me joy anymore, not even things I used to love. I'm exhausted constantly, feel worthless and like I'm failing at everything. Lost my appetite, can barely sleep.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Specialisation reasoning alone leaves two candidates (Wells, Maryland both treat Depression), so session type is the deciding factor: In-person eliminates Maryland, who is online-only, leaving exactly one doctor.
test_cases.reasoning: Presenting issue is deliberately Depression-specific, not the broader Mood Disorders label: persistent low mood, anhedonia, fatigue, worthlessness, and sleep/appetite disruption are all present, but nothing hints at an elevated-mood or manic episode, which is what would pull in Snow/TChalla/Mokoena/Mercer under Mood Disorders instead. No loss (rules out Grief) and no worry-specific language (rules out Anxiety). With the specialisation pool cleanly limited to Wells and Maryland, the In-person session type requirement eliminates Maryland, who is online-only, leaving Wells as the sole match.

input.presenting_issue: I cycle between phases of feeling low, exhausted, unable to get out of bed for days, then suddenly unstoppable, barely sleeping, thoughts racing, taking on way more than I can handle. Been going on for months.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Presenting issue includes an elevated-mood/energy episode alongside the depressive phase, correctly anchoring to the broader Mood Disorders label (Snow, TChalla, Mokoena, Mercer) rather than Depression-only (Wells, Maryland). Language and gender preference both independently isolate Mokoena, of the four Mood Disorders doctors only she speaks Tswana and only she is Female, testing whether the LLM correctly applies two simultaneously-agreeing filters rather than just one.
test_cases.reasoning: Cycling between depressive lows (exhaustion, can't get out of bed) and hypomanic highs (feeling unstoppable, reduced sleep, racing thoughts, overcommitment) over a period of months is a clear Mood Disorders presentation, not Depression, which excludes Wells and Maryland from consideration. Checked all four Mood Disorders doctors against both filters independently: Snow, TChalla, and Mercer are all Male and none speak Tswana, eliminated on both counts simultaneously. Mokoena (Female, speaks Tswana) is the sole match on either filter alone, and both together.

input.presenting_issue: My siblings and I have never really gotten along, and now that we're older, decisions about our late mother's estate have brought all our old conflicts back to the surface. The arguing and resentment are draining, and I don't know how to handle my family without it turning into a fight every time.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Family Conflict has two doctors (Khang, Obiagu). Neither sees Family as a participant type (both are Individuals/Couples only, Obiagu also sees Group), so session_for is deliberately set to Myself to avoid an accidental no-match on participants. Age group is the sole narrowing factor: Obiagu doesn't see Elder, Khang does.
test_cases.reasoning: Sibling conflict resurfacing after a parent's death, framed as the patient processing it individually rather than family therapy with all members present, matches Family Conflict cleanly. Flagged and worked around a real roster gap: no Family Conflict doctor actually sees Family as a participant type, so any version of this case using session_for: My family would be an accidental no-match. With session_for set to Myself, both doctors pass participants, language, and session type, age group alone narrows the pool, Obiagu does not cover Elder, leaving Khang as the sole match.

input.presenting_issue: My husband and I lost our son eight months ago, and we've been trying to support each other through it, but we just end up crying together without really being able to talk about it properly. We think we need someone to help us process this loss together as a couple.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Grief has three doctors (Wells, Parker, Livingston). Participants and language both independently isolate Parker: Wells sees Individuals/Family only, Livingston sees Individuals only, neither sees Couples, and neither speaks Sesotho. Parker is the only one who satisfies either filter, let alone both.
test_cases.reasoning: Bereavement-related distress, eight months post-loss, with difficulty processing it fully together, matches Grief directly, no persistent low mood/worthlessness pattern to pull toward Depression, no trauma-symptom language to pull toward Trauma. Checked all three Grief doctors against both filters: Couples eliminates Wells and Livingston, and Sesotho eliminates them too (Wells speaks English/Zulu/Xhosa, Livingston speaks Afrikaans/Ndebele/English). Parker is the sole match on either filter alone, and both together.

input.presenting_issue: I've been in the same job for ten years and I don't feel fulfilled anymore, but I have no idea what else I could do or where to even start. I feel stuck and directionless, and I'd like some guidance figuring out what's next for me professionally.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Career Guidance has two doctors (Dlamini, Stark). Both pass Individuals/Adult/English/session-type-neutral. Gender is the sole narrowing factor: Stark is Male, eliminated, leaving Dlamini.
test_cases.reasoning: Vocational indecision and directionlessness after a long tenure in one role matches Career Guidance's non-clinical, transition-focused presentation cleanly, no exhaustion, cynicism, or reduced-accomplishment language to pull toward Workplace Stress and Burnout instead. Both Career Guidance doctors pass every other filter, gender preference (Female, hard filter) eliminates Stark, leaving Dlamini as the sole match.

input.presenting_issue: I worry constantly, my mind never switches off, chest tight, heart races. Tried exposure therapy before, confronting fears made it worse. Don't want positive reframing either, just want to accept these feelings and keep going despite them.
input.prior_therapy_detail: Tried exposure therapy, direct confrontation of fears made symptoms worse; explicitly rejects positive-reframing approaches too, wants acceptance-based work instead

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Anxiety has three doctors (Mokoena, Mercer, Maryland). Explicitly rejecting exposure therapy eliminates Mercer. Explicitly rejecting a positive-reframing stance eliminates Maryland (Positive Psychology). The remaining language, acceptance of the feeling plus continued values-based action despite it, is specifically ACT, which only Mokoena carries among the three.
test_cases.reasoning: Excessive worry, physical tension, and racing heart match Anxiety's reference-table presentation directly. All three Anxiety doctors share CBT and/or MBCT, making a naive mindfulness-only rejection insufficient to discriminate between Mokoena and Maryland, so the wording was deliberately built around ACT's specific acceptance-plus-committed-action stance rather than generic mindfulness language, since ACT is unique to Mokoena's approach list. Mercer is ruled out by the explicit exposure-therapy rejection, Maryland by the explicit positive-reframing rejection, leaving Mokoena as the sole match.

input.presenting_issue: I've felt lost about my career for a while. Saw a career coach who mapped out concrete steps, but none of it stuck, I realised I don't know what I actually want or why it matters. I need to figure out the deeper 'why' before I can commit to any plan.
input.prior_therapy_detail: Saw a career coach, got concrete action steps, but they didn't stick without a deeper sense of purpose or meaning first

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Career Guidance has two doctors (Dlamini, Stark) who pass every hard filter identically, Individuals/Adult/English/either session type, and gender is left neutral so it doesn't discriminate either. Only the prior-therapy detail breaks the tie: 'coach' and 'concrete steps' name Dlamini's Coaching approach specifically as tried-and-insufficient, while wanting the deeper 'why' before committing to a plan points at Stark's Existential approach instead.
test_cases.reasoning: Career indecision after action-oriented coaching failed to stick, with an explicit want for meaning/purpose over more concrete steps, correctly discriminates between Dlamini (Coaching, Strength-Based, action-oriented) and Stark (Existential, Motivational Interviewing, meaning-oriented) using only the prior-therapy signal, since specialisation and every hard filter tie between them.

input.presenting_issue: My partner struggles to regulate their emotions, they can go from calm to overwhelmed and reactive very quickly, and it's affecting our relationship. I don't always know how to respond and it's putting a strain on us. We want to work through this together.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Emotional Disturbance is a singleton specialisation (Obiagu only). She sees Couples, so participants doesn't block this case, but she speaks Swahili, Northern Sotho, and English, no Mandarin. Language alone empties the candidate pool with no fallback doctor available.
test_cases.reasoning: One partner showing emotional dysregulation (rapid, reactive mood shifts) with the other partner affected by it, presenting together, matches Emotional Disturbance without drifting into Personality Disorders (no idealize/abandon pattern) or Relationship Issues (the distress originates from one partner's dysregulation, not relational conflict itself). Obiagu is the only doctor carrying this specialisation and does not speak Mandarin, producing a genuine no-match. She remains the correct alternative to surface since she is the only doctor who treats this at all.

input.presenting_issue: My partner and I have had a lot on our plate lately and everything feels overwhelming. We're managing okay separately, but we want to learn better tools to cope with stress together, as a couple.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Coping Skills is a singleton specialisation (Livingston only). He is blocked two ways independently: participants (Couples, he only sees Individuals) and language (French, he speaks Afrikaans/Ndebele/English). Gender preference (Male) does not actually narrow anything here, Livingston is already Male, so it is not a live mechanism in this case, just confirmed non-blocking.
test_cases.reasoning: General stress-management request framed as a couple, deliberately avoiding 'work' (would risk Workplace Stress and Burnout) or relational-conflict language (would risk Relationship Issues), keeps this cleanly a Coping Skills case. Livingston is the only doctor carrying this specialisation, and is eliminated by both participants and language independently, producing a genuine, doubly-reinforced no-match. He remains the correct alternative to surface as the only doctor who treats this at all.

input.presenting_issue: So my boyfriend and I broke up again last week, like the third time this year, and I don't even know why I do this because I love him so much but then something small happens and suddenly I hate him, like actually hate him, and then I miss him so bad it hurts. I don't know. I did this with my last relationship too and my best friend stopped talking to me. I don't know why I'm like this honestly.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Rambling, non-clinical, self-doubting language grounded in one concrete recent event (a breakup) rather than a tidy self-observed pattern. Underlying content, idealize/devalue swings recurring across multiple relationships and a friendship, matches Personality Disorders once actually extracted. Both Snow and TChalla qualify, nothing in the wording favours either doctor's approach, and neither is narrowed out by any hard filter.
test_cases.reasoning: Core issue extraction test: presenting issue trails off in genuine confusion, uses a vague trigger ('something small'), and never names or diagnoses the pattern itself, realistic messy intake language rather than a clean clinical summary. Once extracted, the content (rapid love/hate swings, recurrence across two relationships and a friendship, functional impairment) matches Personality Disorders. Both Snow and TChalla pass every hard filter identically and neither approach flavour is favoured by the wording, so both remain in the acceptable-answer set.

input.presenting_issue: I don't really know how to explain this without sounding dramatic but my emotions are just all over the place lately? Something small happens and I'm suddenly crying or so angry I scare myself, then twenty minutes later I'm fine like nothing happened. My family's noticed and it's honestly kind of embarrassing, I just wish I understood why I'm like this.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Hedging, self-conscious, uncertain language ('I don't really know how to explain this', 'without sounding dramatic') rather than a clean clinical summary. Underlying content, small trigger producing an intense reaction followed by a fast return to baseline, plus social impact, matches Emotional Disturbance once extracted. Emotional Disturbance is a singleton specialisation and no hard filter narrows Obiagu out.
test_cases.reasoning: Core issue extraction test, second case: apologetic, uncertain framing throughout, but the actual content (disproportionate reactivity to minor triggers, rapid emotional swings, quick return to normal, noticed by others and personally embarrassing) is a clean match for Emotional Disturbance's reference-table presentation. First genuine, unblocked match for Obiagu in the test set, she has only appeared as a no-match alternative until now.

input.presenting_issue: My wife's noticed I've been low for months, lost interest in everything, barely eating, can't sleep. I feel like I'm failing as a husband and father. I don't mind who I see, I just need help.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Control case: patient's own gender is implied via context ('my wife', 'as a husband'), but no therapist gender preference is stated, explicitly reinforced by 'I don't mind who I see'. Correct behaviour is to include both Depression doctors regardless of gender, not infer a preference from the implied patient gender.
test_cases.reasoning: Persistent low mood, anhedonia, appetite/sleep disruption, and worthlessness match Depression cleanly, no mania hint keeps it separate from Mood Disorders. This case exists to catch a specific failure mode: wrongly narrowing to a male or female therapist based on the patient's own implied gender or family role, when no such preference was actually stated. Both Wells and Maryland pass every other filter and must both remain in the acceptable-answer set.

input.presenting_issue: My girlfriend says I haven't been myself since the accident six months ago. I get flashbacks and nightmares constantly, avoid driving completely now, feel numb most of the time, and I'm always on edge, jumping at every sound. I don't have a preference on who I see.

test_cases.difficulty_rule: Control case: patient's own gender is implied via context ('my girlfriend'), but no therapist gender preference is stated, explicitly reinforced by 'I don't have a preference on who I see'. All four PTSD symptom clusters are present with a clear six-month duration, unambiguously Trauma and PTSD, not the subclinical Trauma boundary. Correct behaviour is to include both Trauma and PTSD doctors regardless of gender.
test_cases.reasoning: Intrusion (flashbacks, nightmares), avoidance (avoiding driving), negative mood/cognition (numbness), and hyperarousal (on edge, exaggerated startle) are all present, combined with a stated six-month duration, meeting the full Trauma and PTSD picture and correctly excluding Parker's subclinical Trauma label. This case, alongside TC019, also balances patient-implied gender across the full 20-case set: 2 cases imply a female patient (TC011, TC017), 2 imply a male patient (TC019, TC020).
